question:
7. Prove that there are no integers \( x \) and \( y \) for which
\[
x^{2}+3 x y-2 y^{2}=122
\]
solution:
Solution. Multiply (1) by 4 and complete the square to obtain
\[
(2 x+3 y)^{2}-17 y^{2}=488
\]

If (1) has a solution in integers, then so does (2) and hence so does the congruence
\[
u^{2} \equiv 488(\bmod 17) .
\]

But (3) has no solutions because 488 is not a quadratic residue of 17 . The most direct way to see this is to check the possibilities \( u=0,1, \ldots, 8 \). (We need not worry about \( u=9, \ldots, 16 \) because \( u^{2} \equiv(17-u)^{2} \).) Alternatively we can calculate using the quadratic reciprocity theorem and the Legendre symbol. We have
\[
(488 / 17)=(12 / 17)=(2 / 17)^{2}(3 / 17)=(3 / 17)=(17 / 3)=(2 / 3)=-1 ;
\]
hence 488 is not a quadratic residue of 17 .
Remark. We could equally well argue that a solution of (1) and hence of (2) in integers would imply that 17 is a quadratic residue of 61 , since 61 divides 488 . But \( (17 / 61)=(61 / 17)=(10 / 17)=(2 / 17)(5 / 17)= \) \( 1 \cdot(17 / 5)=(2 / 5)=-1 \).

For the quadratic reciprocity theorem and the Legendre symbol see, for example, W. J. LeVeque, Topics in Number Theory, vol. 1, AddisonWesley, Reading, Mass., 1956, page 66 ff.

descriptive_long.question:
7. Prove that there are no integers \( integerx \) and \( integery \) for which
\[
integerx^{2}+3 integerx integery-2 integery^{2}=122
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Multiply (1) by 4 and complete the square to obtain
\[
(2 integerx+3 integery)^{2}-17 integery^{2}=488
\]

If (1) has a solution in integers, then so does (2) and hence so does the congruence
\[
modularu^{2} \equiv 488(\bmod 17) .
\]

But (3) has no solutions because 488 is not a quadratic residue of 17 . The most direct way to see this is to check the possibilities \( modularu=0,1, \ldots, 8 \). (We need not worry about \( modularu=9, \ldots, 16 \) because \( modularu^{2} \equiv(17-modularu)^{2} \).) Alternatively we can calculate using the quadratic reciprocity theorem and the Legendre symbol. We have
\[
(488 / 17)=(12 / 17)=(2 / 17)^{2}(3 / 17)=(3 / 17)=(17 / 3)=(2 / 3)=-1 ;
\]
hence 488 is not a quadratic residue of 17 .
Remark. We could equally well argue that a solution of (1) and hence of (2) in integers would imply that 17 is a quadratic residue of 61 , since 61 divides 488 . But \( (17 / 61)=(61 / 17)=(10 / 17)=(2 / 17)(5 / 17)= \) \( 1 \cdot(17 / 5)=(2 / 5)=-1 \).

For the quadratic reciprocity theorem and the Legendre symbol see, for example, W. J. LeVeque, Topics in Number Theory, vol. 1, AddisonWesley, Reading, Mass., 1956, page 66 ff.

garbled_string.question:
7. Prove that there are no integers \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \) for which
\[
qzxwvtnp^{2}+3 qzxwvtnp hjgrksla-2 hjgrksla^{2}=122
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Multiply (1) by 4 and complete the square to obtain
\[
(2 qzxwvtnp+3 hjgrksla)^{2}-17 hjgrksla^{2}=488
\]

If (1) has a solution in integers, then so does (2) and hence so does the congruence
\[
bnmxvzqe^{2} \equiv 488(\bmod 17) .
\]

But (3) has no solutions because 488 is not a quadratic residue of 17 . The most direct way to see this is to check the possibilities \( bnmxvzqe=0,1, \ldots, 8 \). (We need not worry about \( bnmxvzqe=9, \ldots, 16 \) because \( bnmxvzqe^{2} \equiv(17-bnmxvzqe)^{2} \).) Alternatively we can calculate using the quadratic reciprocity theorem and the Legendre symbol. We have
\[
(488 / 17)=(12 / 17)=(2 / 17)^{2}(3 / 17)=(3 / 17)=(17 / 3)=(2 / 3)=-1 ;
\]
hence 488 is not a quadratic residue of 17 .
Remark. We could equally well argue that a solution of (1) and hence of (2) in integers would imply that 17 is a quadratic residue of 61 , since 61 divides 488 . But \( (17 / 61)=(61 / 17)=(10 / 17)=(2 / 17)(5 / 17)= \) \( 1 \cdot(17 / 5)=(2 / 5)=-1 \).

For the quadratic reciprocity theorem and the Legendre symbol see, for example, W. J. LeVeque, Topics in Number Theory, vol. 1, AddisonWesley, Reading, Mass., 1956, page 66 ff.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
f(x,y)=x^{2}+3xy-2y^{2}, \qquad (x,y\in\mathbb Z),\qquad 
\Delta(f)=17 .
\]

Fix a non-zero integer  
\[
N,\qquad \gcd(N,17)=1,\qquad  
N=\prod_{i=1}^{s}p_{i}^{\,e_{i}}\;(e_{i}\ge 1),
\]
where the $p_{i}$ are pairwise distinct odd primes.

For every odd prime $p\neq 17$ put  
\[
\left(\dfrac{17}{p}\right)=
\begin{cases}
\;1 &\text{if }17\text{ is a quadratic residue modulo }p,\\[6pt]
-1 &\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Define the two index sets  
\[
I=\Bigl\{\,i\mid \bigl(\tfrac{17}{p_{i}}\bigr)=-1\Bigr\},
\qquad
I^{c}=\{1,\dots ,s\}\setminus I .
\]

1. (Global $\Longrightarrow$ local parity obstruction)  
   Show that if  
   \[
   f(x,y)=N
   \]
   has an integral solution, then  
   \[
   e_{i}\equiv 0\pmod{2}\qquad\text{for every }i\in I .
   \tag{P}
   \]

2. (Local-global principle for the form $f$)  
   Prove the converse of (P).  
   Treat the two cases $N>0$ and $N<0$ separately, and make essential use of the following facts about the quadratic field  
   \[
   K=\mathbb Q(\sqrt{17}):
   \qquad h^{+}(K)=1,\qquad
   \varepsilon=33+8\sqrt{17}\text{ is a totally positive unit of norm }1,
   \qquad
   \tau =4+\sqrt{17}\text{ has norm }-1 .
   \]
   Deduce  
   \[
   f(x,y)=N\text{ is solvable in }\mathbb Z^{2}
   \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
   e_{i}\equiv 0\pmod 2\text{ for every }i\in I .
   \tag{$\diamondsuit$}
   \]

3. (Purely local reformulation)  
   Prove that $(\diamondsuit)$ is equivalent to  

   ``for every prime power $p^{k}$ the congruence  
   $f(x,y)\equiv N \pmod{p^{k}}$ is solvable.''

   Hence verify that $f$ satisfies the Hasse-Minkowski principle.

4. Apply $(\diamondsuit)$ to decide whether the equation  
   \[
   x^{2}+3xy-2y^{2}=122
   \]
   possesses integral solutions.

5. (``One-bit'' obstruction)  
   Show that the weaker parity test
   \[
   \sum_{i\in I} e_{i}\text{ odd}\;\Longrightarrow\;
   f(x,y)=N\text{ has no integral solution},
   \]
   thereby recovering the classical criterion, and explain why it is
   insufficient in the present context.

\bigskip
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we set  
\[
K=\mathbb Q(\sqrt{17}),\qquad 
\mathcal O_{K}=\mathbb Z[\omega],\; \omega=\tfrac{1+\sqrt{17}}{2}.
\]

Step 0.  A norm form equivalent to $f$.  
Define  
\[
g(x,y)=x^{2}+xy-4y^{2}.
\]
The unimodular substitution $(x,y)\mapsto(x+y,y)$ yields $g(x+y,y)=f(x,y)$, so $f$ represents $N$ over $\mathbb Z$ iff $g$ does.  
Furthermore  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(x+y\omega)=(x+y\omega)(x+y\bar\omega)=g(x,y).
\tag{0}
\]

Step 1.  Necessity of the parity condition (P).  
Assume $f(x_{0},y_{0})=N$.  Then $N=g(x_{0}+y_{0},y_{0})
     =N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\alpha_{0})$ with $\alpha_{0}=x_{0}+y_{0}\omega\in\mathcal O_{K}$.  

Let $p$ be inert, i.e.\ $\bigl(\tfrac{17}{p}\bigr)=-1$.  
Then $(p)$ remains prime in $\mathcal O_{K}$, say $(p)=\mathfrak P=\bar{\mathfrak P}$, and for every $\beta\in\mathcal O_{K}$ we have  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\beta)\bigr)
      =2\,v_{\mathfrak P}(\beta)
\]
so is even.  Applying this to $\beta=\alpha_{0}$ gives  
$v_{p}(N)=e_{p}$ even, establishing (P).

Step 2.  Sufficiency of (P).  

2.1  Ideal factorisation.  
Write  
\[
N=\Bigl(\prod_{i\in I}p_{i}^{e_{i}}\Bigr)
   \Bigl(\prod_{j\in I^{c}}p_{j}^{e_{j}}\Bigr).
\]
For every $j\in I^{c}$ choose one of the two prime ideals above $p_{j}$ and
call it $\mathfrak p_{j}$.  
For $i\in I$ the only prime ideal is $\mathfrak p_{i}=(p_{i})$.  
Because $e_{i}$ is even, put $e_{i}=2m_{i}$.  Set  
\[
\mathfrak a:=\prod_{i\in I}\mathfrak p_{i}^{\,m_{i}}
            \prod_{j\in I^{c}}\mathfrak p_{j}^{\,e_{j}},
\qquad
(N)=\mathfrak a\,\bar{\mathfrak a}.
\tag{1}
\]

2.2  Principality in the \emph{narrow} class group.  
Since $h^{+}(K)=1$, the ideal $\mathfrak a$ is generated by a
\emph{totally positive} element; choose $\beta\in\mathcal O_{K}$ with  
\[
\mathfrak a=(\beta),\qquad
\beta\succ 0.
\]
Taking norms in (1) gives  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\beta)=u\cdot |N|,\qquad u\in\mathcal O_{K}^{\times},\quad
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(u)=\pm1 .
\]
Because $\beta$ is totally positive, its norm is positive, forcing
$N_{K/\mathbb Q}(u)=1$.  Hence  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\beta)=|N|.
\tag{2}
\]

2.3  Adjusting the sign.  
Let $\tau:=4+\sqrt{17}$; then $N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\tau)=-1$.  Define  
\[
\alpha=
\begin{cases}
\beta &\text{if }N>0,\\[6pt]
\tau\beta &\text{if }N<0.
\end{cases}
\]
In both cases $\alpha\in\mathcal O_{K}$ and  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\alpha)=N.
\tag{3}
\]

2.4  Returning to quadratic forms.  
Write $\alpha=x+y\omega$ with $x,y\in\mathbb Z$.  
By (0) and (3) we have $g(x,y)=N$, whence  
\[
f(x-y,y)=N,
\]
so $(x-y,y)$ is an integral solution.  
Thus (P) $\Longrightarrow$ solvability, completing the proof of $(\diamondsuit)$.

Step 3.  Local solvability and the Hasse-Minkowski principle.  

3.1  $(\diamondsuit)\Rightarrow$ local solvability.  
An integral solution gives one modulo every $p^{k}$.

3.2  Local solvability $\Longrightarrow$ (P).  
Assume that \emph{for every prime power $p^{k}$} the congruence  
\[
f(x,y)\equiv N\pmod{p^{k}}
\tag{4}
\]
is solvable.  Fix an inert prime $p$ and pass to the
$p$-adic completion.  As $f$ is unimodularly equivalent to the norm form
$g$, (4) yields an element $\alpha\in\mathcal O_{K}\otimes\mathbb Z_{p}$
with  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\alpha)=N.
\tag{5}
\]

Let $v_{p}$ be the $p$-adic valuation on $\mathbb Q_{p}$ and extend it
to $K\otimes\mathbb Q_{p}$ (there is a \emph{unique} extension because
$p$ is unramified in $K$).  For every $\beta$ in this local field
\[
v_{p}\bigl(N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\beta)\bigr)=2\,v_{p}(\beta).
\tag{6}
\]
Indeed, the residue field extension is still degree $2$, hence unramified,
so the uniformiser of $\mathcal O_{K}\otimes\mathbb Z_{p}$ is a uniformiser
of $\mathbb Z_{p}$, and the norm multiplies valuations by $2$.

Applying (6) to $\alpha$ in (5) we get  
\[
v_{p}(N)=2\,v_{p}(\alpha)\equiv 0\pmod 2 .
\]
Therefore $e_{p}=v_{p}(N)$ is even for every inert $p$, i.e.\ (P) holds.

Consequently ``complete local solvability'' is equivalent to (P).
Together with Step 2 this proves that $f$ satisfies the
Hasse-Minkowski principle.

Step 4.  Application to $N=122=2\cdot 61$.  
Compute  
\[
\left(\dfrac{17}{2}\right)=1,\qquad
\left(\dfrac{17}{61}\right)=
\left(\dfrac{61}{17}\right)=
\left(\dfrac{10}{17}\right)=
\left(\dfrac{2}{17}\right)\left(\dfrac{5}{17}\right)=1\cdot(-1)=-1.
\]
Hence $61$ is inert and occurs with exponent $1$, violating (P).  
By $(\diamondsuit)$ the equation  
\[
x^{2}+3xy-2y^{2}=122
\]
has \emph{no} integer solutions.

Step 5.  The ``one-bit'' obstruction.  
From Step 1, if $f$ represents $N$ then every inert prime occurs with an
\emph{even} exponent, so  
\[
\left(\dfrac{N}{17}\right)
   =\prod_{i\in I}\left(\dfrac{17}{p_{i}}\right)^{e_{i}}
   =(-1)^{\sum_{i\in I}e_{i}}=1 .
\]
Thus an odd \emph{total} number of inert prime factors already prevents
solvability, reproducing the classical criterion.  However this is
strictly weaker than (P): parity has to be checked \emph{prime by prime}
(e.g.\ $N=15=3\cdot5$ fails $(\diamondsuit)$ although the total number
of inert primes is even).

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  The original (and kernel) problems required only a single congruence
   argument modulo one prime; the enhanced variant demands a full local–global
   characterisation of the values taken by the quadratic form.

•  To prove (*), one must
   – transform a binary quadratic form into a norm form,  
   – work in the quadratic integer ring ℤ[√17],  
   – use the unit group of that ring (via the Pell equation) to lift local
     information to an integral solution, i.e. employ Dirichlet’s “Pell
     trick’’/Hasse–Minkowski type reasoning,  
   – combine these observations with quadratic reciprocity.

•  Part (**) intertwines congruence conditions modulo 4 and 17, demanding
   simultaneous control of two quadratic symbols and multiplicativity; this
   yields an infinite family of forbidden integers rather than a single
   numerical instance.

•  Altogether the enhanced problem obliges the solver to master
   local solvability, norm forms, the structure of units in real quadratic
   fields, the multiplicativity of Legendre symbols, and quadratic reciprocity
   – a substantial escalation in both technical depth and conceptual
   breadth compared with the single-step residue-check of the kernel task.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
f(x,y)=x^{2}+3xy-2y^{2}, \qquad (x,y\in\mathbb Z),\qquad 
\Delta(f)=17 .
\]

Fix a non-zero integer  
\[
N,\qquad \gcd(N,17)=1,\qquad  
N=\prod_{i=1}^{s}p_{i}^{\,e_{i}}\;(e_{i}\ge 1),
\]

For every odd prime $p\neq 17$ put  
\[

\begin{cases}

\end{cases}
\]

\[
I=\Bigl\{\,i\mid \bigl(\tfrac{17}{p_{i}}\bigr)=-1\Bigr\},

   \[

   \qquad h^{+}(K)=1,\qquad

   \qquad
   \tau =4+\sqrt{17}\text{ has norm }-1 .
   \]

   \[
   f(x,y)=N\text{ is solvable in }\mathbb Z^{2}
   \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
   e_{i}\equiv 0\pmod 2\text{ for every }i\in I .
   \tag{$\diamondsuit$}
   \]

3. (Purely local reformulation)  
   Prove that $(\diamondsuit)$ is equivalent to  

   ``for every prime power $p^{k}$ the congruence  
   $f(x,y)\equiv N \pmod{p^{k}}$ is solvable.''

   Hence verify that $f$ satisfies the Hasse-Minkowski principle.

4. Apply $(\diamondsuit)$ to decide whether the equation  
   \[
   x^{2}+3xy-2y^{2}=122
   \]
   possesses integral solutions.

5. (``One-bit'' obstruction)  
   Show that the weaker parity test
   \[
   \sum_{i\in I} e_{i}\text{ odd}\;\Longrightarrow\;
   f(x,y)=N\text{ has no integral solution},
   \]
   thereby recovering the classical criterion, and explain why it is
   insufficient in the present context.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we set  
\[
K=\mathbb Q(\sqrt{17}),\qquad 
\mathcal O_{K}=\mathbb Z[\omega],\; \omega=\tfrac{1+\sqrt{17}}{2}.
\]

Step 0.  A norm form equivalent to $f$.  
Define  
\[
g(x,y)=x^{2}+xy-4y^{2}.
\]
The unimodular substitution $(x,y)\mapsto(x+y,y)$ yields $g(x+y,y)=f(x,y)$, so $f$ represents $N$ over $\mathbb Z$ iff $g$ does.  
Furthermore  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(x+y\omega)=(x+y\omega)(x+y\bar\omega)=g(x,y).
\tag{0}
\]

Step 1.  Necessity of the parity condition (P).  
Assume $f(x_{0},y_{0})=N$.  Then $N=g(x_{0}+y_{0},y_{0})
     =N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\alpha_{0})$ with $\alpha_{0}=x_{0}+y_{0}\omega\in\mathcal O_{K}$.  

Let $p$ be inert, i.e.\ $\bigl(\tfrac{17}{p}\bigr)=-1$.  
Then $(p)$ remains prime in $\mathcal O_{K}$, say $(p)=\mathfrak P=\bar{\mathfrak P}$, and for every $\beta\in\mathcal O_{K}$ we have  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\beta)\bigr)
      =2\,v_{\mathfrak P}(\beta)
\]
so is even.  Applying this to $\beta=\alpha_{0}$ gives  
$v_{p}(N)=e_{p}$ even, establishing (P).

Step 2.  Sufficiency of (P).  

2.1  Ideal factorisation.  
Write  
\[
N=\Bigl(\prod_{i\in I}p_{i}^{e_{i}}\Bigr)
   \Bigl(\prod_{j\in I^{c}}p_{j}^{e_{j}}\Bigr).
\]
For every $j\in I^{c}$ choose one of the two prime ideals above $p_{j}$ and
call it $\mathfrak p_{j}$.  
For $i\in I$ the only prime ideal is $\mathfrak p_{i}=(p_{i})$.  
Because $e_{i}$ is even, put $e_{i}=2m_{i}$.  Set  
\[
\mathfrak a:=\prod_{i\in I}\mathfrak p_{i}^{\,m_{i}}
            \prod_{j\in I^{c}}\mathfrak p_{j}^{\,e_{j}},
\qquad
(N)=\mathfrak a\,\bar{\mathfrak a}.
\tag{1}
\]

2.2  Principality in the \emph{narrow} class group.  
Since $h^{+}(K)=1$, the ideal $\mathfrak a$ is generated by a
\emph{totally positive} element; choose $\beta\in\mathcal O_{K}$ with  
\[
\mathfrak a=(\beta),\qquad
\beta\succ 0.
\]
Taking norms in (1) gives  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\beta)=u\cdot |N|,\qquad u\in\mathcal O_{K}^{\times},\quad
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(u)=\pm1 .
\]
Because $\beta$ is totally positive, its norm is positive, forcing
$N_{K/\mathbb Q}(u)=1$.  Hence  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\beta)=|N|.
\tag{2}
\]

2.3  Adjusting the sign.  
Let $\tau:=4+\sqrt{17}$; then $N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\tau)=-1$.  Define  
\[
\alpha=
\begin{cases}
\beta &\text{if }N>0,\\[6pt]
\tau\beta &\text{if }N<0.
\end{cases}
\]
In both cases $\alpha\in\mathcal O_{K}$ and  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\alpha)=N.
\tag{3}
\]

2.4  Returning to quadratic forms.  
Write $\alpha=x+y\omega$ with $x,y\in\mathbb Z$.  
By (0) and (3) we have $g(x,y)=N$, whence  
\[
f(x-y,y)=N,
\]
so $(x-y,y)$ is an integral solution.  
Thus (P) $\Longrightarrow$ solvability, completing the proof of $(\diamondsuit)$.

Step 3.  Local solvability and the Hasse-Minkowski principle.  

3.1  $(\diamondsuit)\Rightarrow$ local solvability.  
An integral solution gives one modulo every $p^{k}$.

3.2  Local solvability $\Longrightarrow$ (P).  
Assume that \emph{for every prime power $p^{k}$} the congruence  
\[
f(x,y)\equiv N\pmod{p^{k}}
\tag{4}
\]
is solvable.  Fix an inert prime $p$ and pass to the
$p$-adic completion.  As $f$ is unimodularly equivalent to the norm form
$g$, (4) yields an element $\alpha\in\mathcal O_{K}\otimes\mathbb Z_{p}$
with  
\[
N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\alpha)=N.
\tag{5}
\]

Let $v_{p}$ be the $p$-adic valuation on $\mathbb Q_{p}$ and extend it
to $K\otimes\mathbb Q_{p}$ (there is a \emph{unique} extension because
$p$ is unramified in $K$).  For every $\beta$ in this local field
\[
v_{p}\bigl(N_{K/\mathbb Q}(\beta)\bigr)=2\,v_{p}(\beta).
\tag{6}
\]
Indeed, the residue field extension is still degree $2$, hence unramified,
so the uniformiser of $\mathcal O_{K}\otimes\mathbb Z_{p}$ is a uniformiser
of $\mathbb Z_{p}$, and the norm multiplies valuations by $2$.

Applying (6) to $\alpha$ in (5) we get  
\[
v_{p}(N)=2\,v_{p}(\alpha)\equiv 0\pmod 2 .
\]
Therefore $e_{p}=v_{p}(N)$ is even for every inert $p$, i.e.\ (P) holds.

Consequently ``complete local solvability'' is equivalent to (P).
Together with Step 2 this proves that $f$ satisfies the
Hasse-Minkowski principle.

Step 4.  Application to $N=122=2\cdot 61$.  
Compute  
\[
\left(\dfrac{17}{2}\right)=1,\qquad
\left(\dfrac{17}{61}\right)=
\left(\dfrac{61}{17}\right)=
\left(\dfrac{10}{17}\right)=
\left(\dfrac{2}{17}\right)\left(\dfrac{5}{17}\right)=1\cdot(-1)=-1.
\]
Hence $61$ is inert and occurs with exponent $1$, violating (P).  
By $(\diamondsuit)$ the equation  
\[
x^{2}+3xy-2y^{2}=122
\]
has \emph{no} integer solutions.

Step 5.  The ``one-bit'' obstruction.  
From Step 1, if $f$ represents $N$ then every inert prime occurs with an
\emph{even} exponent, so  
\[
\left(\dfrac{N}{17}\right)
   =\prod_{i\in I}\left(\dfrac{17}{p_{i}}\right)^{e_{i}}
   =(-1)^{\sum_{i\in I}e_{i}}=1 .
\]
Thus an odd \emph{total} number of inert prime factors already prevents
solvability, reproducing the classical criterion.  However this is
strictly weaker than (P): parity has to be checked \emph{prime by prime}
(e.g.\ $N=15=3\cdot5$ fails $(\diamondsuit)$ although the total number
of inert primes is even).

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  The original (and kernel) problems required only a single congruence
   argument modulo one prime; the enhanced variant demands a full local–global
   characterisation of the values taken by the quadratic form.

•  To prove (*), one must
   – transform a binary quadratic form into a norm form,  
   – work in the quadratic integer ring ℤ[√17],  
   – use the unit group of that ring (via the Pell equation) to lift local
     information to an integral solution, i.e. employ Dirichlet’s “Pell
     trick’’/Hasse–Minkowski type reasoning,  
   – combine these observations with quadratic reciprocity.

•  Part (**) intertwines congruence conditions modulo 4 and 17, demanding
   simultaneous control of two quadratic symbols and multiplicativity; this
   yields an infinite family of forbidden integers rather than a single
   numerical instance.

•  Altogether the enhanced problem obliges the solver to master
   local solvability, norm forms, the structure of units in real quadratic
   fields, the multiplicativity of Legendre symbols, and quadratic reciprocity
   – a substantial escalation in both technical depth and conceptual
   breadth compared with the single-step residue-check of the kernel task.
